tools.description: Close a GitHub issue with a closing comment. Use this when work is complete, the issue is no longer relevant, or it's a duplicate. The closing comment should explain the resolution or reason for closing. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be closed. Target: *.

body.description: Closing comment explaining why the issue is being closed and summarizing any resolution, workaround, or conclusion.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to close. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 901 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/901). If omitted, closes the issue that triggered this workflow (requires an issue event trigger).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

state_reason.description: The reason for closing the issue. Use 'COMPLETED' for resolved issues, 'NOT_PLANNED' for issues that won't be addressed, or 'DUPLICATE' for duplicate issues. Defaults to 'COMPLETED'.

tools.name: close_issue

tools.description: Add a comment to an existing GitHub issue, pull request, or discussion. Use this to provide feedback, answer questions, or add information to an existing conversation. For creating new items, use create_issue, create_discussion, or create_pull_request instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 2 comment(s) can be added. Target: *.

body.description: The comment text in Markdown format. This is the 'body' field - do not use 'comment_body' or other variations. Provide helpful, relevant information that adds value to the conversation.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: The issue, pull request, or discussion number to comment on. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 123 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/123). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run. If omitted, the tool will attempt to resolve the target from the current workflow context (triggering issue, PR, or discussion). If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for this comment. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Auto-generated if not provided. The temporary ID is returned in the tool response so you can reference this comment later.

tools.name: add_comment

tools.description: Add labels to an existing GitHub issue or pull request for categorization and filtering. Labels must already exist in the repository. For creating new issues with labels, use create_issue with the labels property instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be added. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to add labels to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, adds labels to the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to add (e.g., ['bug', 'priority-high']). Labels must exist in the repository. This field is required — omitting it will cause a validation error.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: add_labels

tools.description: Remove labels from an existing GitHub issue or pull request. If a label is not present on the item, it will be silently skipped. Use this to clean up labels that are no longer applicable. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 7 label(s) can be removed. Target: *.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

item_number.description: Issue or PR number to remove labels from. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 456 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/456). If omitted, removes labels from the item that triggered this workflow.

labels.description: Label names to remove (e.g., ['bug', 'needs-triage']). Labels that don't exist on the item are silently skipped. At least one label must be provided.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: remove_labels

tools.description: Assign one or more GitHub users to an issue. Use this to delegate work to specific team members. Users must have access to the repository.

assignee.description: Single GitHub username to assign. Use 'assignees' array for multiple users.

assignees.description: GitHub usernames to assign to the issue (e.g., ['octocat', 'mona']). Users must have access to the repository.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

issue_number.description: Issue number to assign users to. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 543 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/543). If omitted, assigns to the issue that triggered this workflow.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: assign_to_user

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Post a routing comment @mentioning team owners on the triggering issue; bypasses safe-outputs mention neutralization

message.description: The comment body text without any @mentions or @ symbols

owners.description: Comma-separated GitHub usernames to notify, without the @ prefix (e.g. 'user1, user2, Azure/team-name')

tools.name: mention_owners

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

content.text: {"number":57967,"title":"[Azure Management Generator] Docs: Document scope-based extension resource parameter ordering and complex parent path ValidateRe [Content truncated due to length]","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt` **Service directory:** `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/` **Triggered by:** PR #57934 by `@live1206` ## What Changed Two bug fixes were merged for the Azure Management SDK generator/emitter: 1. **Generator (`OperationContext.cs`):** Fixed a LIFO stack push-order bug in `BuildContextualParameterHierarchy`. For scope-based extension resources (e.g., `ChaosTarget`, `KubernetesClusterExtension`), the stack was producing reversed positional order, causing `Id.Name` and `Id.ResourceType.Type` to be swapped in REST call arguments. 2. **Emitter (`resource-metadata.ts`):** Fixed `getExpectedParentResourceType` to handle parent paths with more than 3 segments. For resources scoped under nested multi-provider paths (e.g., `managementGroups/{mgId}/subscriptions/{subId}/providers/Microsoft.Quota/...`), the function now falls back to computing the parent scope resource type from the last `/providers/` segment, enabling `ValidateResourceId` generation for those collections. ## Gaps Found - The `Extension Resources` section in `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/docs/OperationContext.md` (lines 162–168) is a two-sentence placeholder. It does not explain scope-based extension resources, how the `BuildContextualParameterHierarchy` LIFO stack resolves positional parameters for them, or the interaction between `Id.Name` and `Id.ResourceType.Type` for extension resource contexts. - There is no documentation describing how the emitter's `getExpectedParentResourceType` function computes the expected parent resource type for collection resources, the 3-segment rule, or the fallback to the last `/providers/` segment for multi-provider nested scopes. - There is no changelog entry for either fix in the `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/` package — there is no `CHANGELOG.md` at all in that directory. 📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files #### File 1: `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/docs/OperationContext.md` (edit) Expand the **Extension Resources** section (currently lines ~162–168) to cover scope-based extension resources in detail. Replace the current terse section with: ````markdown ### Extension Resources Extension resources have a flexible scope — they can be attached to any Azure resource. Their contextual path starts with a single `resourceUri` variable: ``` /{resourceUri}/providers/Microsoft.Example/extensions/{extensionName} ``` The `resourceUri` parameter is treated as a contextual parameter derived from the parent resource's `Id`. #### Scope-Based Extension Resources Some extension resources also capture the parent resource **type** as a contextual parameter in addition to its name. For example, a `ChaosTarget` contextual path looks like: ``` /{scope}/providers/Microsoft.Chaos/targets/{targetName} ```` When the generator builds `ContextualParameter` objects for such resources, each variable segment in the path is paired with the constant key that precedes it using a LIFO (stack-based) algorithm. The variable pairs are pushed onto the stack in **key-then-value** order so that the resulting positional list matches the path order from outermost to innermost scope. The generated REST call correctly supplies arguments in this order: ````csharp _restClient.Get( Id.Parent.ResourceType.Type, // e.g., "Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters" Id.Parent.Name, // e.g., "myCluster" Id.Name, // e.g., "myTarget" cancellationToken ); ``` If the stack push order were reversed (a previous bug), `Id.Name` and `Id.ResourceType.Type` would be swapped, producing incorrect REST calls. ```` #### File 2: `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/docs/OperationContext.md` (edit) Add a new subsection under **Advanced Scenarios** documenting the emitter's `ValidateResourceId` generation for complex parent paths. Add after the **Singleton Resources** subsection: ````markdown ### ValidateResourceId for Complex Multi-Provider Scopes When generating a resource collection, the emitter computes the **expected parent resource type** to emit a `ValidateResourceId` guard in the `GetAll` method. For simple paths (≤ 3 segments), the parent type is derived directly from the second-to-last provider segment. For nested multi-provider paths with more than 3 segments — such as: ``` managementGroups/{mgId}/subscriptions/{subId}/providers/Microsoft.Quota/quotas ``` the emitter falls back to computing the parent scope resource type from the **last `/providers/`** segment in the path. This ensures that `ValidateResourceId` is emitted correctly for these collection types even when the parent path has complex, multi-level scope nesting. ```` #### File 3: `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/CHANGELOG.md` (create) Create a new CHANGELOG file to track generator/emitter changes: ```markdown # Changelog ## Unreleased ### Bug Fixes - Fixed incorrect parameter ordering for scope-based extension resources (e.g., `ChaosTarget`, `KubernetesClusterExtension`). The LIFO stack in `BuildContextualParameterHierarchy` was reversed, swapping `Id.Name` and `Id.ResourceType.Type` in generated REST client arguments. - Fixed `ValidateResourceId` generation for resource collections whose parent paths contain more than 3 segments (nested multi-provider scopes such as `managementGroups/{mgId}/subscriptions/{subId}/providers/Microsoft.Quota/...`). The emitter now falls back to the last `/providers/` segment to compute the expected parent resource type. ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No README snippet placeholders are needed for these documentation-only changes (the docs are in `.md` files under `docs/`, not in a package README that uses the snippet generation tooling). ### Step 3: Verify README structure The `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/README.md` is an npm package README (not a client library README), so the standard section checklist (Getting Started, Key Concepts, Examples, Troubleshooting, etc.) is not required. No changes needed to `README.md`. ### Step 4: Validate The docs changes are Markdown-only; no build or test commands are required. However, if any `.cs` source was touched, run: 1. `dotnet build eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/generator/Azure.Generator.Management/` 2. `dotnet test eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/generator/Azure.Generator.Management/ --filter TestCategory!=Live` ## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **`@copilot`** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above > Generated by [Update Docs](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/actions/runs/24167755634/agentic_workflow) · ● 434.7K · [◷](https://github.com/search?q=repo%3AAzure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net+is%3Aissue+%22gh-aw-workflow-call-id%3A+Azure%2Fazure-sdk-for-net%2Fupdate-samples-and-docs%22&type=issues) ","state":"open","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/issues/57967","user":{"login":"github-actions[bot]","id":41898282,"profile_url":"https://github.com/apps/github-actions","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/in/15368?v=4"},"author_association":"NONE","reactions":{"total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"hooray":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"created_at":"2026-04-09T01:40:34Z","updated_at":"2026-04-09T01:40:34Z"}

{"total_count":11,"incomplete_results":false,"items":[{"id":4223768142,"number":57931,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"[Management Generator] Docs: Document extension resource detection with specific parent types","body":"## Documentation Gap **Package:** `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt` **Service directory:** `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/` **Triggered by:** Commit [REDACTED] (PR #57722) by `@live1206` ## What Changed PR #57722 fixed a bug in the management generator's resource detection logic to correctly handle **extension resources that target a specific parent resource type** (e.g., a resource that can only extend `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines` rather than any arbitrary resource). The fix touched `resource-detection.ts`, `resource-metadata.ts`, and `resolve-arm-resources-converter.ts`. ## Gaps Found - The existing `docs/OperationContext.md` covers extension resources only for the **generic/wildcard scope** case (paths starting with `/{resourceUri}/providers/...`), but does not explain extension resources that specify a **specific parent resource type** via the TypeSpec `@armResourceOperations` scope, nor how the generator detects and differentiates between the two - There is no documentation explaining **how the generator detects whether an extension resource has a specific parent type vs. a wildcard scope**, and what generated code differs between the two cases (e.g., which extension class the resource's helper methods are generated on) - No CHANGELOG file exists in `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/` to record this bug fix for users of the generator who may have been working around it
📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files #### File 1: `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/docs/OperationContext.md` — **edit** Expand the existing **Extension Resources** subsection (under **Advanced Scenarios**) to cover the specific parent type case. Replace the current section: ````markdown ### Extension Resources Extension resources have a flexible scope (can extend any resource). Their contextual path starts with a single variable segment: ``` /{resourceUri}/providers/Microsoft.Example/extensions/{extensionName} ``` The system handles these specially, treating `resourceUri` as a contextual parameter derived from the parent resource's Id. ```` with: ````markdown ### Extension Resources Extension resources extend another Azure resource. The generator recognizes two sub-types: #### Wildcard-scope extension resources When an extension resource can extend **any** resource type, the operation paths use a single variable prefix: ``` /{resourceUri}/providers/Microsoft.Example/extensions/{extensionName} ``` The system treats `resourceUri` as a contextual parameter derived from the extended resource's `Id`. Helper methods are added to the `ArmClient` mockable extension class so callers can invoke them from any `ArmResource`. #### Specific-parent extension resources When an extension resource is scoped to a **specific parent resource type** (for example, it can only extend `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines`), the TypeSpec definition constrains the scope. The detected parent type determines which Azure scope class (e.g., `ResourceGroupResource`, `SubscriptionResource`, `TenantResource`, `ManagementGroupResource`, or the parent resource's own collection/resource class) receives the helper extension methods. The generator distinguishes these cases during resource detection by inspecting whether the extension resource's path prefix resolves to a known Azure scope path rather than a generic variable segment. When a specific parent is detected, the resource's `parentResourceId` in the code model is set to the concrete parent path, enabling the generator to place helper methods on the correct extension class. **Example:** An extension resource with parent type `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines`: ``` /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}/resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/{vmName}/providers/Microsoft.Example/extensions/{extensionName} ``` The generator detects `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines` as the specific parent and adds `GetExtension(string extensionName)` to the `MockableVirtualMachineResource` class, not the generic `ArmClient` extension. ```` #### File 2: `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/CHANGELOG.md` — **create** Create a CHANGELOG file to track generator releases and bug fixes: ```markdown # Release History ## Unreleased ### Bug Fixes - Fixed detection of extension resources that target a specific parent resource type. Previously, extension resources with a constrained scope (e.g., restricted to `Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines`) were not correctly identified as having a specific parent, causing helper methods to be generated on the wrong extension class or missing entirely. ([#57722](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/pull/57722)) ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No README snippet changes are needed for this developer-tool documentation update. C# Generator] Paging action operations should not resource-wrap return types","body":"## Bug Description The MPG C# generator incorrectly wraps paging results in resource types for **action** operations. When a paging operation is an ARM **action** (not a list), the generator should return the raw data type (e.g., `Pageable`), not the resource-wrapped type (e.g., `Pageable`). **Example TypeSpec:** [ServerConfiguration.tsp](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/blob/main/specification/postgresqlhsc/resource-manager/Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL/PostgresqlHsc/ServerConfiguration.tsp) — the `listByServer` operation is an `ArmResourceActionSync` (action on `ClusterServer`), not a resource list on `ServerConfiguration`. ## tspCodeModel evidence The emitter correctly identifies the operation as an **Action** in the resource metadata: ```json { "methodId": "Microsoft.DBforPostgreSQL.Configurations.listByServer", "kind": "Action", "operationPath": ".../{clusterName}/servers/{serverName}/configurations", "resourceScope": ".../{clusterName}/servers/{serverName}" } ``` However, the method-level metadata only says `kind: "paging"` — the Action vs List distinction is only in the resource operation metadata. ## Expected behavior Since the operation is an **Action** (not a **List**), the generated method should return: ```csharp public virtual Pageable GetConfigurations(...) ``` ## Actual behavior The generator sees the `paging` kind + a matching `NodeConfigurationResource` class, and produces: ```csharp public virtual Pageable GetConfigurations(...) ``` ## Root cause The generator does not check the ARM resource operation `kind` (Action vs List) when deciding whether to resource-wrap paging results. It only checks the method-level `kind: "paging"` and the existence of a matching resource class. ## Current workaround Use `CodeGenSuppress` in a `Customized/` file to suppress the generated method and replace it with a hand-written version returning the raw data type. ## 🎯 Agentic Issue Triage

**Summary:** Documentation gaps were identified in the Azure Management SDK generator/emitter following PR #57934 — the `OperationContext.md` Extension Resources section is a placeholder, and there is no documentation for `getExpectedParentResourceType` fallback logic or a `CHANGELOG.md` in the package.

<details>
<summary>📋 Issue Details</summary>

**Package:** `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt` (generator/emitter in `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/`)
**Affected API:** `BuildContextualParameterHierarchy` (generator), `getExpectedParentResourceType` (emitter), `OperationContext.md` docs
**Scenarios:**
- `OperationContext.md` Extension Resources section (lines 162–168) is a stub; it does not explain scope-based extension resource parameter ordering via the LIFO stack
- No documentation describing how `getExpectedParentResourceType` computes the expected parent type for complex (>3 segment) multi-provider paths
- No `CHANGELOG.md` exists in `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/` to record bug fixes

**Root ask:** Expand `OperationContext.md` Extension Resources section, add a new subsection for complex multi-provider parent path handling, and create `CHANGELOG.md` with entries for the two bug fixes merged in PR #57934.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🔎 Debugging / Reproduction Notes</summary>

This is a documentation-only issue triggered automatically from PR #57934. The underlying code changes (fixes to LIFO stack ordering and `getExpectedParentResourceType` fallback) have already been merged.

**Suggested investigation steps:**
1. Review `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/docs/OperationContext.md` lines 162–168 and expand the Extension Resources section with scope-based extension resource explanation and LIFO stack details.
2. Add a new subsection (e.g., "ValidateResourceId for Complex Multi-Provider Scopes") documenting the `getExpectedParentResourceType` fallback for paths with >3 segments.
3. Create `eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/CHANGELOG.md` with "Unreleased" section and bug fix entries for the two fixes from PR #57934.
</details>

<details>
<summary>🏷️ Label Confidence</summary>

- **Category:** `Mgmt` — The issue is specifically about the Azure Management SDK generator (`http-client-csharp-mgmt`), which targets SDKs in the `Azure.ResourceManager.*` family.
- **Service:** `CodeGen` — The issue is about the code generator/emitter located in `/eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/`; per triage conventions, generator/emitter issues in `/eng/packages/` use the "CodeGen" service label.
- **Confidence:** High — The CODEOWNERS file explicitly has a `# ServiceLabel: %CodeGen %Mgmt` entry for `/eng/packages/http-client-csharp-mgmt/`, directly matching this issue.
</details>
